best_for: Next.js apps (built by the same team, first-class support), or any frontend framework needing zero-config deploys, preview URLs per PR, and edge/serverless functions — the default recommendation for this skill's default stack
avoid_when: Long-running background jobs, WebSocket servers needing persistent connections, or workloads needing more compute/timeout than serverless functions allow
tradeoffs: Fastest path from git push to a globally-distributed deploy with almost no config, native Next.js optimizations, but serverless function timeouts/cold-starts apply and costs scale with usage in ways worth monitoring at high traffic

best_for: Static sites and JAMstack apps across many frameworks (not Next.js-specific), teams wanting a mature, framework-agnostic build/deploy platform with strong forms/functions add-ons
avoid_when: Deep Next.js-specific optimizations (ISR nuances, etc.) where Vercel's first-party support has an edge
tradeoffs: Very similar developer experience to Vercel (git-integrated, preview deploys, edge functions) with a longer track record on static/JAMstack specifically, and a generous free tier for small projects

best_for: Latency-sensitive global apps, or projects wanting to stay within the Cloudflare ecosystem (Workers, D1, KV, R2) for one unified platform and billing surface
avoid_when: Apps needing a traditional Node.js runtime with full npm ecosystem compatibility — Workers run on a V8 isolate, not full Node, and some packages don't work unmodified
tradeoffs: Best-in-class global edge latency and a genuinely generous free tier, but the Workers runtime constraint (no filesystem, limited CPU time per request, partial Node API compatibility) requires checking package compatibility before committing

best_for: Full backend services needing a persistent process (WebSocket servers, long-running workers, traditional Express/Nest.js/Django apps) without managing raw VMs/Kubernetes yourself
avoid_when: A purely frontend or serverless-function-shaped workload — those fit Vercel/Netlify/Cloudflare more naturally and cheaply
tradeoffs: Simpler than raw cloud infrastructure (Docker-based deploys, managed Postgres add-ons, background workers) while still supporting long-running processes that serverless platforms don't, at a higher baseline cost than pure serverless for low-traffic apps

best_for: Teams with specific compliance/data-residency requirements, very high and predictable traffic where reserved infrastructure is cheaper than pay-per-use serverless, or workloads needing full OS-level control
avoid_when: Small-to-mid projects, or teams without dedicated infrastructure/DevOps expertise to maintain it — the operational burden is real and ongoing
tradeoffs: Full control over the runtime environment and potentially lower cost at high sustained scale, but you own patching, scaling, monitoring, and incident response entirely yourself — a significant ongoing cost most projects this skill targets shouldn't take on prematurely

best_for: Any project using Supabase as its BaaS (see backend stacks module) — database, auth, storage, and realtime all managed together with one dashboard and MCP tooling available when connected
avoid_when: A project with no Supabase-platform features in use at all, where a plain managed Postgres (Neon, RDS) is a lighter-weight fit
tradeoffs: Database, auth, storage, RLS-based authorization, and realtime subscriptions all in one managed platform with generous free tier, at the cost of the schema/auth model being somewhat Supabase-shaped (though the underlying Postgres remains fully portable)

best_for: Projects wanting a plain managed relational database with serverless scaling (scale-to-zero, branching for preview environments) without the rest of a BaaS platform's auth/storage bundled in
avoid_when: A project that would benefit from Supabase's bundled auth/storage/RLS integration anyway — those save real integration work over a bare database
tradeoffs: Database branching (a full copy-on-write branch per PR, similar to git) is a standout feature for preview environments, and true serverless scale-to-zero pricing fits spiky/low-traffic workloads well

option: Cloudflare Hyperdrive (pool a traditional DB from Workers)
best_for: Projects on Cloudflare Workers needing to reach a traditional Postgres/MySQL database (not D1) without the connection-pooling problems Workers' request-scoped execution model would otherwise cause
avoid_when: Projects already using D1 (Cloudflare's native SQLite-based edge database) with no need to reach an external database
tradeoffs: Lets Workers reach a normal external Postgres/MySQL with proper connection pooling and reduced latency via Cloudflare's network, bridging the gap between edge compute and traditional databases

option: Platform-Native Object Storage (Supabase Storage, Cloudflare R2, Vercel Blob)
best_for: Any project already committed to that platform's ecosystem — avoids adding a separate cloud storage vendor and separate billing/auth surface for file uploads
avoid_when: A project with very specific requirements (data residency in a specific region not offered by the platform, extreme scale requiring S3-specific tooling) that a platform-native option doesn't meet
tradeoffs: One less vendor to integrate and authenticate against, usually with the platform's own auth/RLS model extending naturally to file access control — R2 specifically has zero egress fees, a meaningful cost difference at scale versus S3

best_for: Projects with existing AWS infrastructure, needing S3-specific ecosystem tooling, or requiring very specific regional/compliance guarantees
avoid_when: A project on Vercel/Supabase/Cloudflare with no other AWS dependency — adding S3 introduces a second cloud vendor, second auth model, and (unlike R2) real egress fees
tradeoffs: The most mature and widely-integrated object storage option with the broadest tooling ecosystem, at the cost of egress fees and being a second vendor/auth surface to manage alongside your primary platform

best_for: Simple scheduled tasks (nightly reports, cleanup jobs) on a platform that supports them (Vercel Cron, Supabase Edge Function cron, Cloudflare Cron Triggers) — avoids standing up separate job infrastructure
avoid_when: Long-running jobs exceeding the platform's function timeout, or jobs needing real queueing/retry/concurrency-control semantics beyond 'run this on a schedule'
tradeoffs: Zero extra infrastructure for simple scheduled work, but most serverless cron implementations have real timeout limits — not a fit for genuinely long-running batch work

option: Managed Queue Service (e.g. a hosted queue/worker platform)
best_for: Real background-job workloads: webhook processing, bulk imports, video/image processing, anything needing retry/backoff, concurrency control, and a dead-letter queue (BE074)
avoid_when: Simple scheduled tasks with no queueing/retry complexity — that's platform-native cron territory, not a full queue service
tradeoffs: Proper job semantics (retries, backoff, DLQ, concurrency limits) that a scheduled function can't give you, at the cost of standing up and paying for a separate piece of infrastructure

best_for: Any project on one of these platforms — static assets and (for Cloudflare specifically) even dynamic responses are cached at the edge automatically with no separate CDN configuration
avoid_when: A specific need for CDN features the platform's bundled CDN doesn't offer (highly custom cache-key logic, a specific enterprise CDN vendor relationship already in place)
tradeoffs: Zero-config edge caching bundled with the hosting platform already in use — genuinely one less piece of infrastructure to think about for most projects

option: Register and Manage DNS on the Same Platform as Hosting (where offered)
best_for: Small teams wanting the least operational surface area — Vercel, Cloudflare, and others offer integrated domain management alongside hosting
avoid_when: An organization with an existing centralized domain-management policy/registrar that all properties must go through regardless of hosting platform
tradeoffs: One fewer account/dashboard to manage and often faster DNS propagation/SSL provisioning when domain and hosting are on the same platform, at the cost of some platform lock-in for DNS specifically

option: Deploy to a Single Region by Default, Add Multi-Region Only With a Measured Need
best_for: The overwhelming majority of projects — start with a single region close to your primary user base and measure actual latency before adding complexity
avoid_when: A genuinely global user base where measured latency data shows a real user-experience problem in specific regions — that's the trigger to add multi-region, not a default assumption
tradeoffs: Single-region is simpler to reason about, cheaper, and avoids multi-region data-consistency complexity (which region is the source of truth for a write) — most projects never actually need multi-region and adding it prematurely is pure complexity tax

best_for: New projects on this skill's default stack (Next.js + Supabase, or similar) — no server to patch, scales to zero, matches the adaptive-stack recommendation in backend-architecture.md
avoid_when: Long-running connections (WebSockets), workloads needing more than a few dozen ms/seconds of CPU per request consistently, or predictable high-sustained-load workloads where reserved compute is cheaper
tradeoffs: No infrastructure to manage and true scale-to-zero cost for low-traffic projects, but function timeout/cold-start characteristics need to be designed around (see BE065) rather than ignored

best_for: WebSocket-heavy real-time apps, workloads with sustained high CPU/memory needs, or anything needing persistent in-memory state across requests
avoid_when: Simple request/response APIs with spiky/low traffic — serverless is both simpler to operate and cheaper there
tradeoffs: Full control over the runtime with no cold starts or per-invocation timeout constraints, but you're responsible for capacity planning, scaling, and process supervision (graceful shutdown, BE075) yourself
